chemicals.chemical_formula: Not provided in the given sources.
chemicals.concentration_notes: Starfruit is highly toxic to cats; even a very small amount or a single bite can cause poisoning34. Kittens (due to their smaller, developing bodies) and adult cats with pre-existing kidney issues are exceptionally vulnerable to the potency of this fruit
chemicals.description: in cats: Unlike insoluble oxalates that primarily cause localized oral irritation, the soluble calcium oxalates found in starfruit are absorbed directly into the cat's bloodstream2. Once in the blood, these compounds bind to systemic calcium, causing a sudden and dangerous drop in calcium levels (hypocalcemia)2. This process severely interferes with kidney function and can rapidly lead to acute ki
chemicals.name: Soluble calcium oxalates

description: Starfruit (Averrhoa carambola) is severely toxic to cats and dogs, though most research has focused on cats and humans with kidney disease. It contains caramboxin — a neurotoxic compound that inhibits glutamate decarboxylase — and very high oxalate concentrations that cause kidney injury. Even small amounts can be life-threatening.
name: Starfruit
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: caramboxin (neurotoxin) and high soluble oxalates; the entire fruit is toxic
Mechanism: caramboxin inhibits GABA synthesis, causing neurological excitation; oxalates precipitate as calcium oxalate crystals in kidney tubules
Symptoms: vomiting, hiccups, agitation, confusion, seizures, acute kidney failure; onset within 1–5 hours
There is no known safe amount of starfruit for cats — even a small piece or juice can cause toxicity
Any starfruit ingestion is a veterinary emergency

symptoms.name: Vomiting, drooling, and loss of appetite
symptoms.notes: These are typical early signs of starfruit poisoning that alert an owner to toxicity before more severe systemic issues become obvious1. 2. Increased Thirst, Dehydration, Bloody Urine, and Kidney Failure

symptoms.name: Increased thirst and urination, dehydration, bloody urine, and eventually reduced urine output or a complete inability to urinate
symptoms.notes: The soluble calcium oxalates in starfruit are absorbed into the bloodstream and severely interfere with kidney function, rapidly leading to acute kidney injury or fatal renal failure if not treated promptly1.... Extreme lethargy and an inability to urinate are signs of late-stage kidney failure1. 3.

symptoms.name: Lethargy and weakness
symptoms.notes: Lethargy can present as an early symptom of toxicosis and will progress to extreme lethargy as the cat experiences severe kidney failure1. 4. Seizures

symptoms.name: Seizures
symptoms.notes: Seizures can occur as a severe systemic reaction to the toxicity, driven by hypocalcemia (a dangerous drop in blood calcium levels) which happens when the soluble oxalates bind to calcium in the cat's bloodstream

treatments.description: The veterinarian will medically induce vomiting to empty the cat's stomach and remove the starfruit before the toxins can be fully digested and absorbed into the body
treatments.name: Emesis Induction
treatments.notes: This treatment is highly time-sensitive and is typically only performed if the ingestion was recent2. You should never attempt to induce vomiting at home unless explicitly instructed to do so by a vet. 2. Toxin Binding

treatments.description: Following emesis, the veterinarian will administer a dose of activated charcoal to bind to and absorb any remaining toxins left in the gastrointestinal tract
treatments.name: Activated Charcoal Administration
treatments.notes: This step is crucial for preventing the highly toxic soluble calcium oxalates from crossing the intestinal lining and entering the cat's bloodstream2. 3. Renal Protection and Stabilization

treatments.description: The veterinary team will admit the cat to administer IV fluids, which helps protect the kidneys by flushing the system, while also monitoring and stabilizing the cat's overall condition
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy and Supportive Care
treatments.notes: This ongoing supportive care is essential because the soluble oxalates in starfruit bind to systemic calcium, causing a sudden and dangerous drop in blood calcium levels (hypocalcemia)13. If not aggressively managed with fluid therapy and supportive care, this process can quickly lead to irreversibl
